text: The video shows gross sacculation of the small bowel due to chronic stricturing associated with Crohn's disease. The terminal ileum and right colon are visibly affected, reflecting common regions of disease activity.

A fifty-eight year old man presented to his family doctor with a thirty year history of abdominal cramps and intermittent bloating, predominantly postprandially. Upper GI endoscopy was unremarkable but ulcers were noted in the terminal ileum and a granuloma seen in a rectal biopsy. Small bowel stricturing was identified on CT. A diagnosis of stricture in Crohn's disease was made but the patient refused surgery. Subsequently, he sought attention as his symptoms had deteriorated over a year. He had no other medical history of note and was then referred to our institution. An MR enterogram showed multiple distal ileal strictures with proximal gross sacculation. No enhancement was seen in the strictures, suggesting chronicity.

During the laparoscopy, the surgeon performs a full small bowel examination from the ileocecal junction to the DJ junction, identifying several dense stricturing masses in the mid to distal ileum, along with gross chronic sacculation of the intervening segments.

The surgeon delivers the diseased segment of the small bowel through an extended umbilical port site. The atonic sacculated segments between the strictures are visibly enlarged, measuring eleven centimeters in diameter. A single resection of the strictured segments is performed, followed by a side-to-side stapled anastomosis.

A second small bowel walk was carried out at a relaparoscopy after the anastomosis was constructed. The patient made an unremarkable post-operative recovery At outpatient follow-up six months later, he reported a substantial improvement in quality of life and had gained five kilograms in weight. Pathological examination of the specimen showed patchy chronic inflammation with mucosal ulceration, fissuring ulceration and transmural lymphoid aggregates. Non-caseating granulomata were seen at submucosal and intramuscular locations.
